summary: I went west right after the war in 1867 and traveled up the Missouri River all the way to Fort Benton, Montana. The river was a major route for us heading into the frontier. It was a time when the Blackfeet Indians were very active along the river, and the whole region was wild and full of opportunity.

Fort Benton was the end of the line for me on the Missouri River trip in 1867. It was a bustling place with mining camps and saloons open day and night. The Blackfeet were nearby, and the town was full of all sorts of characters, from miners to road agents. I remember seeing five road agents hanged there one day, including a man named George Ives.

Back in 1867, the Blackfeet Indians were out in full force near Fort Benton, Montana. Their chief, Raven Claw, had two thousand warriors, and they were a formidable presence. I had to be careful around them, especially when trapping in the wilderness, as they could be quite hostile.

George Ives was one of the road agents I saw hanged at Fort Benton. He was caught and put up on a pile of barrels with a rope around his neck. When he started to wiggle like a fish out of water, his pal Boon Hallam told him to cheer up because they'd be in Hell together soon. That was a harsh time with some strange characters roaming around.

Liver-Eating Johnson was one of the strangest characters I ever met out west. He was a giant of a man, powerful and mostly kept to himself, but when he drank, he could get noisy and destructive without meaning to. The story goes that he got his name after killing some attacking Indians while trapping and eating the liver of a young one because he liked liver best. He was also known for carrying a leg of his dead partner on his saddle to eat on the way back to the fort.

I heard a story from a man named Mr. X who claimed to have been in Alaska just before or after the gold rush, though not in 1898. He described the brutal cold winters where your breath would freeze and even crush your toes if it built up. He and a few others got trapped in the mountains prospecting and had to endure the harsh winter with little food, losing a young man named Jimmy to the cold. They had to freeze Jimmy upright in the ground because they couldn't dig a grave.

The gold rush was a time of great excitement and danger. Mr. X talked about being in Alaska around that period, prospecting in the mountains during a brutal winter. It was a time when many men came seeking fortune, but the conditions were so harsh that some, like Jimmy, didn't survive. The gold rush brought a mix of hard men and dreamers to the frontier.

Dolan, a man I knew, claimed he had built Pike's Peak. He was a rough character who had done many jobs and traveled widely. When people heard he built Pike's Peak, they sent for him to come to California to build Yosemite Valley as well. He said he finished Yosemite Valley in three days but was then thrown out as a no-good bum.

According to Dolan, after building Pike's Peak, he was coaxed to California to build Yosemite Valley. He claimed to have finished the job in three days, but despite that, the locals called him a bum and threw him out. It was part of his rough and tumble life as a modern hobo who had done many odd jobs.

Paul Maurana was mentioned as someone who sensationalized the idea of a 'tavism' in Negro blood, suggesting a primitive ancestral worship. My narrator, an elderly Negro woman from the South, found his claims misleading and pernicious. She told me stories that showed a rich tradition of supernaturalism but also a skepticism toward superstition, which is more cultural than racial.

Dolan used the term 'Hoosier' not to mean people from Indiana, but as a way to call someone a hick or naive. He was contemptuous of those who wanted to settle down and live a quiet farm life, saying they would save for forty years to buy twenty acres. He believed that if these 'Hoosiers' traveled more, they might learn something, showing his disdain for their narrow outlook.
